turns.content: Hey! I just started building a proof-of-concept where the main thread fetches data from an API and pushes it to worker threads via mpsc channels. It works, but I'm wondering if there's a better way than manually managing these channels every time.

That's a solid start! You're right that mpsc is the go-to for many producer-consumer patterns in Rust. But since you're already using async, maybe you should consider if tokio::sync::watch or oneshot would fit your specific flow better.

I'm not sure what the difference is between mpsc and watch channels. I thought mpsc was just for sending messages, but then I heard about bounded vs unbounded too. Does that mean it only works with mpsc?

Great question! Think of tokio::sync::mpsc like a mailbox where you can drop items without waiting if it's full, but tokio::sync::watch is more like a shared notebook that only lets you update the same page. Watch channels are especially useful when one side needs to keep track of state changes from another.
Oh, so watch channels aren't just for sending messages? I thought they were similar to mpsc but with less backpressure. Can I use them instead of mpsc in my current setup?

Exactly! If your main thread only needs to notify workers about state changes rather than streaming a lot of data, watch channels are perfect. They handle backpressure differently too since they're designed for shared mutable state.
That makes sense. But what if I need both sending many messages and tracking the latest value? Can I combine them?
You can absolutely! A common pattern is to use mpsc for heavy data flow and watch channels alongside it for state notifications. This gives you the best of both worlds without losing performance.
I see. So mpsc handles the bulk, while watch keeps things updated efficiently? Is there a way to avoid using multiple channel types altogether?

Sometimes yes, depending on your use case. If you only need simple send-receive without complex state tracking, mpsc might be enough. But for anything involving shared mutable data between async tasks, watch channels are often the cleaner choice.
Alright, so if I'm still using mpsc right now but want to simplify things later, should I switch to watch? What about oneshot or broadcast?

Oneshot is great for one-time sends where the receiver doesn't care about more. Broadcast channels are useful when multiple consumers need to receive updates from a single sender. But if you're doing continuous communication like your current setup, watch or mpsc will likely serve you better.
Thanks! I think I'll stick with mpsc for now but keep an eye on watch channels in case my requirements evolve. Does anyone have any tips on how to choose the right one?

Here's a simple rule: if you need continuous streaming of data, use mpsc. If you're sharing state between tasks and want efficient updates, go with watch channels. And remember, bounded vs unbounded depends on whether you want backpressure or not.
Why does this even work this way? I thought channels were just for passing data, but why do we need different types like mpsc and oneshot when a simple queue seems enough?

The confusion comes from assuming channels are only about transport. In tokio, the choice of channel type dictates how you handle concurrency and safety without manual synchronization. The mpsc channel allows multiple producers and consumers to communicate safely because it uses a ring buffer internally with internal locks that you don't need to manage, whereas oneshot is designed for fire-and-forget scenarios where you want exactly one value sent once.
So I can just use a regular std::sync::mpsc channel in an async context too? That sounds like what I'm trying to do but with async runtime constraints.

That's the wrong assumption. You cannot simply drop std::sync::mpsc channels into an async runtime because they block on synchronous operations and don't integrate with tokio's executor. The async equivalents are tokio::sync::mpsc for bounded buffers that respect backpressure, or tokio::sync::broadcast for one-to-many patterns where consumers can subscribe to the same stream without needing a separate sender instance.
Okay so if I need multiple senders and receivers in my frontend backend logic, which one should I pick?

If your scenario involves multiple producers pushing data into a shared buffer that gets consumed by multiple async tasks, you should use tokio::sync::mpsc. It's the idiomatic choice because it combines bounded capacity with backpressure support via send limits and internal locking, preventing deadlocks that happen when you try to manually coordinate synchronization across threads in a single-threaded async runtime.
But what if I only need one sender but many receivers? Like broadcasting updates from the server to all connected clients?

For that specific pattern where you have exactly one source pushing data and multiple independent consumers reading it, you should use tokio::sync::broadcast. It's designed specifically for this asymmetry and doesn't require you to manage senders or handle capacity limits manually since the broadcast receiver automatically handles synchronization internally.
I still don't get why oneshot exists if I can just use a channel with drop logic?

Oneshot is unique because it enforces exactly one receive at the consumer level without relying on external cleanup. When you create an oneshot, the sender side drops automatically after sending once, and the receiver blocks until that single value arrives. This guarantees termination of a specific operation without needing complex error handling or manual state management around sender lifetimes.

I'm trying to contribute to this open-source project but I'm stuck on why the async logic feels so weird compared to Java. In my old code I had a single thread processing requests, but here everything seems to run in parallel and then there's some buffering magic happening that I don't understand yet.

Welcome to the project! That feeling of 'why does this work this way' is very common when switching from Java concurrency models. In Rust with tokio, we use channels like mpsc or oneshot to communicate between tasks instead of shared memory, which avoids data races entirely.
Oh I see, so there's no shared state? But wait, if I just send a message through an mpsc channel won't it queue up forever since there's no explicit limit on how many messages can be stored?

Good catch! That's exactly what makes bounded channels valuable. An unbounded channel would indeed grow infinitely, but mpsc has a configurable capacity that acts like backpressure. When the queue fills up, senders are paused until space opens up, preventing memory exhaustion.
Ah okay so I can set a max size? But what if I need to receive just one message and stop there immediately without waiting?
Perfect scenario for oneshot channels. They are designed specifically for that one-time transfer where the sender is dropped after sending, so the receiver gets exactly one value and nothing else.
That sounds great but what if I'm listening to multiple sources like a file system watcher or network sockets all at once?

For that use case you'd want broadcast channels. They allow one sender to notify many receivers simultaneously, which is ideal for event-driven architectures where a single source updates multiple consumers.
Okay so mpsc for queues, oneshot for signals, and broadcast for fans? But what about when I need to track specific events over time like a timer?
That's where watch channels come in. They are specifically designed for tracking changes on variables or timers without needing to poll, making them perfect for monitoring state updates.
Wait so I'm basically choosing between these four based on whether I need backpressure, one-time delivery, multiple consumers, or event tracking?
Exactly! Your choice depends entirely on the communication pattern. If you're building a request handler pipeline, mpsc with bounded capacity usually works best for handling load.
I've been reading about async Rust and heard a lot about channels, but I'm not sure which one to use. What's the difference between mpsc, oneshot, broadcast, and watch channels?

That is a great starting point because choosing the right channel type depends entirely on what you need from your communication pattern. The mp sc channel is designed for many-to-many communication where multiple producers can send to multiple consumers, so it uses a bounded buffer internally which means if the consumer isn't fast enough, new messages will block until space opens up—that's called backpressure and it prevents data loss by slowing down the sender naturally. On the other hand, the oneshot channel is simpler because you only get one message per sender, making it perfect for signaling completion or sending a single result without needing to handle multiple senders or receivers. The broadcast channel is useful when you want many consumers receiving from a single producer, and watch channels are specifically designed for observing values that change over time in an async context.
That helps clarify the basic types, but I'm still confused about how they fit into real scenarios. If I have two services exchanging data continuously, which one should I pick?

For continuous exchange between multiple producers and consumers, you would typically use an mpsc channel because it supports many-to-many communication patterns effectively. Since it has a bounded buffer size by default, if your consumer service can't keep up with the incoming messages from the producer services, the sender will block until there is space available in the queue, which automatically creates backpressure and prevents memory issues.
Okay, so mpsc handles multiple senders and receivers. But what if I only need to send a single result once after an operation completes?

In that case, you should use oneshot channels because they are specifically designed for one-off communication where you want to guarantee the message is delivered exactly once and then discarded. The key difference is that mp sc allows multiple sends while oneshot only permits a single send per sender instance.
I see. But what if I have a single producer but many consumers, like logging or metrics collection?

For a single producer with many consumers, you would use the broadcast channel since it allows one sender to communicate with multiple receivers simultaneously without needing separate channels for each pair of sender and receiver.
And what about when I need to observe state changes over time, like tracking user counts?

For observing values that change over time, you would use watch channels because they are specifically designed for async scenarios where you want to receive notifications whenever a value changes without needing manual polling or complex synchronization logic.
So the main difference seems to be about many-to-many versus one-off versus single-to-many versus observation. Is there another way to think about this?

You could think of it in terms of direction and constraints: mpsc is many producers to many consumers with backpressure, oneshot is one sender to one receiver with no repeated sends, broadcast is one producer to many consumers, and watch is for observing stream changes asynchronously.
That makes sense as a categorization. But I'm still wondering about the bounded buffer aspect in mpsc specifically.

The bounded buffer in mp sc ensures that if your consumer isn't fast enough, the sender will block until there is space available, which creates backpressure and prevents data loss by slowing down the sender naturally.
So if I set the capacity too low, the system could just hang forever?

Yes, exactly. If you set the capacity too low and your consumer cannot keep up with the producer, the sender will block indefinitely until space opens up, which can cause the entire program to hang unless you handle that blocking behavior explicitly.
That's a critical point I didn't realize before. So backpressure is actually a safety mechanism?

Yes, backpressure acts as a safety mechanism by preventing unbounded memory growth when consumers fall behind producers, so it is essential to understand the capacity constraints you set for your channels.
I think I'm getting clearer now. But I have one more question about how these compare to regular std::sync channels.

Tokio channels are specifically designed for async contexts and support non-blocking operations by default, whereas std::sync channels block on the thread that tries to send or receive, which is less efficient in async environments.
I've read about mpsc and oneshot channels but I still don't fully understand how they differ in my CI/CD pipeline context. I thought if I just used a regular channel it would work fine.

That's a common misconception. Regular channels aren't async-safe, so you can't use them inside tokio tasks without deadlocks or race conditions. For your pipeline where jobs need to be processed concurrently, you should definitely use mpsc channels since they allow multiple producers and consumers.
But I don't understand why bounded is so important even with mpsc. My current code just uses an unbounded channel and the CI keeps failing under load.

Exactly, that's the backpressure issue you're facing. When a consumer can't keep up with producers on an unbounded channel, messages get lost or tasks block forever. Bounded channels limit capacity and automatically reject excess sends when full, which prevents your pipeline from overflowing.
So I should just replace my current channel setup with mpsc bounded? But what about oneshot - do I ever use that in async pipelines?

Oneshot is perfect for one-time communication patterns like sending a result back to a single caller after a long computation. In your CI scenario, you might use it when a build step completes and needs to notify exactly one downstream task about success or failure without any queueing.
I'm confused about broadcast channels too. Should I replace mpsc with that for my parallel worker pool?

No, don't use broadcast for your worker pool unless you specifically need multiple consumers to receive the same message simultaneously like in a pub/sub system. Your workers probably just need independent access to different messages, which mpsc handles better with proper backpressure.
But what about watch channels? I saw them mentioned somewhere and thought they might help me track build progress in real time.

Watch channels are useful when you need to observe whether a task is still running or has completed without manually polling. However, for your CI pipeline where jobs send results forward, mpsc with bounded capacity is the right choice since watch doesn't support multiple producers naturally.
Wait so I should be using mpsc bounded channel for my main job queue and maybe oneshot for notifications? But how do I actually implement this in my code?

You create the sender and receiver with tokio::sync::mpsc_channel, set capacity to something like 100 based on your worker count, then spawn async tasks that send jobs through the sender while workers pull from the receiver. For notifications you can use a separate oneshot channel per job completion.
I'm still not clear on how to handle backpressure properly in my code. The CI keeps timing out when I try to process more jobs than workers can handle.

The key is ensuring your sender respects the bounded capacity limit. When the channel fills up, tokio automatically rejects further sends until space opens up. You just need to make sure your producer doesn't try to queue more than what workers can actually consume in real time.
Ah so I shouldn't be buffering my own messages before sending them through the channel? That would defeat the purpose of backpressure?

Correct, you should send jobs directly to the mpsc sender without additional local queues. The bounded channel itself provides the buffer and rejection mechanism you need. If you add extra buffering before the channel, you're masking the real constraint instead of solving it.

Wait I think I understand now - my CI was failing because I was trying to queue more jobs locally than my workers could process. By using mpsc bounded properly, each send attempt either succeeds or gets rejected gracefully instead of causing a deadlock.

Exactly! That's the aha moment - backpressure isn't just about performance it prevents your pipeline from breaking under load. Your code should now use tokio::sync::mpsc_channel with appropriate capacity, send jobs directly without extra buffering, and handle rejected sends when the channel is full.

I was reviewing your new worker service implementation and I'm noticing you're using a lot of blocking operations in the hot path. In Java, we usually rely on CompletableFuture chains or reactive streams to handle this, so why are you reaching into tokio channels here?

Good catch, I was trying to avoid the overhead of full async/await chains for simple data passing. I implemented a shared worker pool where each consumer gets its own mpsc channel to receive tasks, and producers write to a broadcast channel so they don't need to know about individual consumers.

Wait, broadcasting is dangerous if you have too many consumers because it can cause memory pressure. In Java we typically use publish/subscribe patterns with backpressure handling built into the reactor framework, not just raw channels. Why did you choose broadcast over mpsc for this scenario?

You're right that broadcast has risks, but in my case I needed to notify all workers of a global shutdown event without locking the entire pool. I used oneshot channels for termination signals since they only allow one receiver per sender which prevents race conditions during cleanup.

Actually, I think you misunderstood the problem here. If multiple workers need to receive the same data simultaneously, broadcast isn't ideal because it lacks backpressure and will fill up memory. You should have used a bounded mpsc channel with a reasonable capacity instead.

I see what you mean now. I did set the capacity to 1000 messages which provides some backpressure, but I didn't realize it would overflow under high load. Can you explain how tokio channels handle backpressure differently than Java's reactive streams?

Tokio channels are simpler because they're just buffered queues. When the buffer fills up, send operations block until space opens up. In contrast, Java's reactive streams use operators like buffer() and debounce() to manage flow more explicitly.

That makes sense, so I should probably switch to mpsc with a smaller capacity for the task queue while keeping oneshot only for shutdown signals. Can you show me how to properly configure the channel builder in tokio?
Just use spawn_blocking or spawn async with the channel type specified, like mpsc::channel(50). I'd also recommend wrapping it in a Mutex if you need shared access to channel metadata.

I'll update my code to use mpsc channels for task distribution and keep oneshot for the shutdown signal. One more thing though, how does tokio handle multiple senders writing to the same broadcast channel concurrently?

Broadcast is essentially a one-to-many pattern where each sender can write independently but receivers are limited. If you really need concurrent writes from many sources, consider using a mutex-protected queue or a message bus crate instead.

Thanks for clarifying that distinction. I'll refactor my code to use mpsc for task distribution and oneshot only for termination signals, removing the broadcast channel entirely since it's not needed here anymore.

I'm working on a new feature where a controller service needs to push updates to multiple client workers. I've been using simple blocking queues in my prototype, but now I need to rewrite this in async Rust with tokio. How do I set up channels so that the controller can broadcast updates to all active worker threads without them competing for CPU time or causing deadlocks?

For broadcasting updates to multiple workers, you should use a Broadcast Channel from tokio. It's designed specifically for this scenario where one sender needs to notify many receivers simultaneously. You can create it with `tokio::sync::broadcast` and send messages using the `.send()` method on the channel handle.

Okay, so I'll set up a Broadcast Channel for my controller service. But what if some of my workers are slow or occasionally unresponsive? How do I ensure that when they're not ready to receive an update, the sender doesn't get stuck waiting forever?

That's exactly why you should use a Broadcast Channel instead of a regular channel for this pattern. The receivers in a Broadcast Channel can be polled independently and won't block each other. If your workers are slow, they just won't consume messages until they're ready, but the sender will keep sending to all subscribers without waiting on any single one.
I see. So if I have ten worker threads and send an update every second, they'll each get a copy? What happens if my controller crashes or needs to stop sending updates suddenly?

Yes, all active subscribers will receive the same update. If your controller stops sending, the existing channel buffer will eventually fill up based on its capacity setting you chose when creating it. Once full, new sends will fail unless you handle errors or drain old messages.
I'm worried about that buffer overflow though. Should I always use a very large capacity? Or is there a way to make the channel automatically adjust its size based on how fast workers are consuming?

You shouldn't rely on automatic resizing for critical production data. Instead, think of this like a traffic cop at an intersection: if cars (messages) arrive faster than they can leave through the lanes (consumers), you either slow down the arrival rate or add more lanes. In Rust terms, you choose a reasonable buffer size when creating your channel and handle overflow by either rejecting messages or implementing backpressure logic in your sender.
Got it. But I'm still confused about the difference between mpsc channels which I've seen before, and this broadcast pattern we're using here now. Can you help me understand when to use each type?

An mpsc channel is like a single-lane road where only one car can pass at a time between two specific points; it's perfect for point-to-point communication. A Broadcast Channel is more like a public address system in an office building: one person speaks, but everyone hears them simultaneously without needing to coordinate who goes next.
That analogy makes sense! So mpsc would be for when my controller needs to talk to exactly one worker thread at a time, right? And broadcast is only for the multi-worker scenario we're building?

Exactly. If your design requires sequential processing where each message goes to exactly one receiver, mpsc is ideal since it prevents duplicate sends and manages ordering naturally. But if you need simultaneous delivery to multiple independent consumers, that's when Broadcast Channels shine.
One more thing though: what about oneshot channels? Do I ever use those in this kind of architecture?

Oneshot channels are great for fire-and-forget scenarios where you only need to send one message and then forget it. For example, if your controller needs to signal a shutdown event to exactly one worker once and never communicate again with that specific worker afterward, oneshot is perfect since it guarantees the sender won't block waiting for confirmation.
And watch channels? I've heard those mentioned in async docs but don't have a clear picture of how they fit into this controller-worker pattern we're building?

Watch Channels are essentially a way to observe the state changes of another channel without directly sending or receiving messages through it. They're useful for monitoring whether your workers have completed their tasks or if certain conditions have been met, letting you react to those events asynchronously without breaking the main communication flow.

So I should probably start with a Broadcast Channel setup since that's what our architecture requires. But before committing to the final design, what are some common pitfalls I should watch out for when implementing this in production?

The biggest pitfall is forgetting to handle send failures gracefully. Since Broadcast Channels can fill up, you need error handling on every send operation. Another issue is ensuring your workers properly drain the channel when shutting down so old messages don't pile up indefinitely in memory.

I think I understand now. One final question: if I later decide to add more complex features like rate limiting or priority queuing, can these channels support that easily, or do I need completely different primitives?

You don't necessarily need entirely new primitives. You can layer logic on top of the basic channel types by implementing custom senders or using middleware patterns within your application code to enforce rate limits or manage priorities before messages reach the actual channels.
I'm nervous about my Rust interview because I keep confusing sync and async, kind of like how I used to mix up threads and processes before they stopped working together.

That's a great way to think about it. The core reason you need tokio channels is because in async Rust, tasks run concurrently without blocking the main thread. If your code tries to share state directly between threads or tasks using mutable variables, you get race conditions where one task overwrites data another just read.
So I should just use shared memory instead of channels because it's faster?

You can't share mutable state directly between async tasks in Rust without using the Send trait and proper synchronization, which is exactly why tokio provides these specialized channel types. The real problem with shared memory here is that it requires explicit locking mechanisms like mutexes inside your functions, which defeats the purpose of non-blocking async code by introducing blocking waits.
I still think I should try to avoid channels if possible since they add overhead.

That's a common misconception because channels do have allocation costs, but the alternative of using locks or raw shared memory creates much higher contention overhead in your concurrent tasks. The tokio mpsc channel handles backpressure automatically by buffering messages until space is available, whereas manual synchronization would block one task while waiting for another to finish.
Can you explain why bounded channels are better than unbounded ones?

Bounded mpsc channels prevent your producer from flooding the consumer because they enforce a limit on how many messages can be stored in memory. This backpressure ensures that if a task is slow, the fast tasks won't overwhelm it with data, which keeps your system responsive rather than letting one bottleneck drag down everything else.
What about oneshot channels? When would I ever use those?

Oneshot is perfect when you need to send a single value from one task to another and then forget it, like shutting down a worker thread or sending a final result. It guarantees the sender can only write once and the receiver gets exactly one message with no possibility of duplicates.
And broadcast channels? I thought those were just for groups?

Broadcast channels in tokio are specifically designed so multiple receivers can listen to the same stream of data without copying it into separate buffers. This is useful when you have several workers that need to process updates from a single source without duplicating memory usage across all of them.
What about watch channels? Are those different?

Watch channels are like a bidirectional version where you can both send and receive events, making them ideal for coordination between tasks that need to monitor each other's state changes in real time.
So I should definitely use mpsc for my interview example since it handles the most common case?

Yes, mpsc is your go-to choice because it's designed specifically for many producers and one consumer with automatic backpressure. When you explain that in an interview, showing how you chose bounded over unbounded based on expected message volume demonstrates deep understanding rather than just knowing the syntax.
Hey, I'm new to this codebase and I'm confused about the channels. Is mpsc just a simpler version of broadcast?

Not really. mpsc is for many-to-many communication where both sender and receiver can be multiple, while broadcast specifically allows one sender to push to many receivers simultaneously. The main difference is that mpsc has backpressure limits based on capacity whereas broadcast doesn't care about the number of senders.
Ah okay so I should use mpsc for everything since it handles multiple senders automatically?

You don't need to choose between them based on sender count alone. If your receiver is a single consumer that needs to stop when the stream ends, you should use oneshot instead of mpsc because it guarantees the message gets delivered exactly once.
Got it but what about watch? Is that just another name for mpsc?

No, watch is different because it's designed for reactive patterns where you want to observe changes in a value over time. While mpsc sends discrete messages, watch allows receivers to subscribe and receive updates when the data changes without needing to poll.
So if I'm building a notification system for multiple users, should I use broadcast or maybe just send through oneshot channels?

For notifications where one event triggers multiple receivers at once, broadcast is the right choice. But remember that bounded mpsc is what you want when you need backpressure to prevent overwhelming your receiver with too many messages at once.
That makes sense. So I should always use bounded channels in production code?

Not always, but it's a good default for performance. Unbounded channels can cause memory issues if your producer outpaces the consumer indefinitely. Bounded channels let you set limits and handle backpressure naturally.
What happens if I forget to add the capacity parameter when creating a bounded channel?
If you omit the capacity, tokio will default to an unbounded channel which can lead to memory exhaustion. Always specify a reasonable limit like 100 or 1000 depending on your expected message volume.

I'm onboarding to a new service and I keep hitting the mpsc limit when my worker threads try to send requests. My code uses a bounded channel with capacity 10, but suddenly the whole system stops accepting connections because the sender saturates. Should I just increase the buffer size or switch to an unbounded option?

Increasing the buffer is a quick fix for this specific symptom, but it's not the right architectural decision if you expect high throughput. The real issue here is that your producer isn't draining the queue fast enough, causing backpressure to kill the connection. You should switch to a mpsc channel with a much higher capacity or implement a flow control pattern where the sender stops when the receiver lags.

That makes sense about the saturation point, but what if I need to send requests that can't be queued at all? Like when an incoming request arrives and my current worker is still processing a previous one?

In that scenario where you cannot buffer the request because it would block the producer, you should use oneshot channels to forward individual requests immediately. The oneshot channel ensures the sender gets an immediate response and the receiver processes one request at a time without worrying about queueing delays.
But my application needs to handle many concurrent incoming connections, not just one per worker. Can I use oneshot for that?

No, you cannot use oneshot channels when handling multiple concurrent requests because each oneshot channel is single-use and connects only two parties. For handling many incoming connections concurrently, you need a broadcast channel or a watch channel to notify all workers that new work is available.
Wait, I thought watch channels were just for monitoring state changes like in Reactor patterns? Can they actually handle the request dispatching I described?

You're right that watch channels are primarily designed for observing events rather than distributing work. If you need to distribute incoming requests among multiple workers, stick with mpsc or broadcast channels instead of using watch channels for dispatching.
So I should use a broadcast channel to notify all my worker threads when new requests arrive? Is there any downside to that approach?

Yes, using broadcast channels has downsides. While they allow multiple receivers, they don't support backpressure like mpsc does, so if your producers keep sending faster than workers can consume, the channel will saturate and block new connections just like your initial problem.
Then what's the best pattern for this? Do I need to implement a custom flow control mechanism or is there a built-in way in tokio?

The best approach is to use mpsc channels with appropriate capacity settings combined with a per-worker request queue. You can leverage the backpressure built into mpsc by keeping your worker threads actively draining their queues rather than just blindly sending requests.

I'm completely stuck on this tutorial about async channels and I can't figure out why my producer thread is hanging. Here's the code: let (tx, rx) = mpsc::channel::<i32>(10); loop { let val = 42; tx.send(val).await?; }

Hey, don't panic. The issue is that your consumer never actually runs or consumes the data, so the buffer fills up and blocks the sender. You need a separate task to read from rx. Try creating a tokio::spawn for the receiver loop.
Oh right, I forgot about spawn. But now my consumer is just sitting there doing nothing even though it has an await on send. Why isn't it getting values?
Because you're awaiting inside the receive loop without checking if data arrives first. Make sure your receiver logic checks for completion or uses a while loop properly instead of just one await.
Wait, I think I'm confusing mpsc with oneshot. The tutorial says to use oneshot for some cases but why would I ever need that if mpsc seems so flexible?
Good question. Use oneshot when you only need exactly one message and want the sender to be dropped automatically after delivery, like signaling completion once an item is done.
That makes sense for a single notification but what about broadcasting updates to multiple consumers? I keep seeing broadcast mentioned in docs.
Right, use tokio::sync::broadcast if you need one sender pushing to many receivers simultaneously without copying data around too much.
Okay so broadcast is like mpsc but with multiple receivers? And what about watch channels?
Yes, similar idea but watch channels are specifically designed for observing state changes in variables without manually polling them.
So if I have a running service that updates every second and needs to notify various UI threads, which one should I pick?
For that use case, broadcast is probably best since it handles many subscribers efficiently without duplicating the entire message payload.
Wait, what about bounded vs unbounded? I'm confused when to set a capacity limit on my channel.
Set bounded when you want backpressure to prevent overwhelming resources or flooding the system with too many messages at once.
So if my buffer fills up, the sender automatically pauses until space opens? That sounds like exactly what I need for rate limiting.
Exactly. The channel acts as a backpressure mechanism naturally without needing extra code to manage flow control manually.
One more thing though, how do I handle errors when sending fails? Like what if the buffer is full and tx.send returns an error?
If you set a bounded channel with capacity N, sending beyond that will fail. You should check the result of send() and handle the Full error appropriately in your sender loop.
